item.title: Judicial independence: One step forward, two steps back. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen-3-32B, GPT-OSS-120B

During the first session of the parliament formed through the February 12 elections, over a hundred ordinances promulgated by the immediate past transitional government of Professor Muhammad Yunus were vetted into full acts. However, several proposals failed to clear the legislative hurdle. On March 12, 2026 -- the opening day of the 13th parliament's first session -- a total of 133 ordinances were tabled. A parliamentary special committee recommended letting certain proposals lapse or remain in abeyance for further stakeholder vetting. By the close of the session, 97 ordinances were passed completely unchanged, 13 were adopted with amendments, and 23 were either dropped, lapsed, or shelved. Of the ordinances that failed to secure parliamentary approval, the ones concerning judicial independence sparked the most intense public debate. Citizens were understandably surprised by an apparent rollback of measures widely believed to have paved the way for an autonomous judiciary. When the Yunus-led government promulgated the Supreme Court Secretariat Ordinance, 2025, late last year, public aspirations ran high. The law was expected to cement institutional independence by placing administrative authority squarely in the hands of the Chief Justice. Instead, the new government passed a repeal bill to re-evaluate this framework, triggering a sharp debate over the delayed implementation of a separate, autonomous administrative body. Following the ordinance's promulgation in November 2025, then-Chief Justice Syed Refaat Ahmed had inaugurated the Supreme Court Secretariat on December 11, hailing the moment as a historic institutional milestone. The current Law, Justice, and Parliamentary Affairs Minister, Md Asaduzzaman -- who served as the Attorney General at the time -- attended that ceremony. He stood alongside Justice Refaat as the then chief justice formally unveiled the secretariat by removing the ceremonial cloth from the cornerstone at Supreme Court Administrative Building-4. One can remember the Chief Justice declaring that day: "Our journey does not end here. The most important step ahead is to ensure the continuity and effective functioning of this secretariat." Unfortunately, within four months that “continuity” comes to a halt. Law Minister Md Asaduzzaman moved a bill in parliament to repeal that very ordinance. On April 9, the Jatiya Sangsad passed the Supreme Court Secretariat (Repeal) Bill, 2026, effectively dismantling the nascent independent secretariat for the country’s highest court. This sudden reversal has fueled widespread concern over the future of judicial autonomy in Bangladesh. A fully independent judiciary serves as the primary mechanism for upholding the rule of law, protecting fundamental human rights, and maintaining a democratic system of checks and balances. Article 22 of the Bangladesh Constitution reads: “The State shall ensure the separation of the judiciary from the executive organs of the State.” While this Article mandates the separation of the judiciary from the executive, achieving total institutional and functional independence remains an indispensable prerequisite for true democratic governance. Seeking to calm public anxiety, the government has urged patience, promising a more refined framework following deeper stakeholder consultations. In justifying the repeal, officials reasoned that further scrutiny is required to determine the ideal structure and necessity of a separate Supreme Court Secretariat. Nevertheless, what prompted the government to completely abolish the freshly minted secretariat remains a mystery to many. As a direct consequence of the repeal, all posts specifically created for the secretariat have been scrapped. Furthermore, the budgets, projects, and programs previously managed by the judiciary's independent arm have been transferred back to the executive's Law and Justice Division. Without its own administrative secretariat, the judiciary remains heavily reliant on the Ministry of Law, Justice, and Parliamentary Affairs. This structural dependence severely limits institutional autonomy, tying day-to-day administrative functions to the executive branch. Essential operational needs -- such as budget allocations, financial allowances, and courtroom resources -- remain subject to executive approval. Now that the interim-era ordinance has been repealed to make room for re-evaluation, the focus shifts to how swiftly the government rolls out this new consultation process. For Bangladesh, this marks yet another delay in a long, exhausting journey that began with the historic Masdar Hossain judgment. The 1999 Masdar Hossain case legally separated the subordinate judiciary from the executive branch in 2007. However, persistent systemic loopholes have consistently restricted absolute autonomy. The ongoing push for a separate secretariat focuses entirely on detaching judicial administration from the Ministry of Law to secure true financial and administrative freedom. An independent court acts as the ultimate guardian of the constitution, ensuring that the legislature and the executive do not exceed their constitutional bounds. It provides a secure avenue for citizens to seek remedies against the abuse of state power. Courts cannot protect fundamental rights if they remain vulnerable to external pressures. True independence prevents the powerful from dominating the weak, ensuring that legal disputes are decided strictly on their merits rather than through political or financial influence. Reaz Ahmad is Editor, Dhaka Tribune.

item.title: Tourism is not just about nature. It is design. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Qwen-3-32B

The engine of the long-tail boat softened as we drifted away from Maya Bay into the darkening Andaman Sea. Behind us, the limestone cliffs had already swallowed the last light of sunset. Ahead, Phi Phi was beginning to appear out of the darkness -- first a few scattered yellow lights, then entire lines of glowing beach bars along the shore. Around the boat, the sea had turned almost black. Occasionally, flashes of plankton shimmered beside the wood whenever the waves touched the sides. The boatman steering us through the water introduced himself as Lek. “Too many tourists before,” he said quietly, pointing back toward the darkness where Maya Bay had disappeared. “Coral damaged. Water changed. Now [the] government controls more.” He kept one hand loosely on the engine handle as he spoke, barely looking at the water. He did not need to. The route seemed to live somewhere inside his body already. For “Lek” the boatman, the place was not paradise. It was work. The sea behind us -- the same sea glowing beneath the boat -- had once nearly been destroyed by the crowds it attracted. The island lights ahead existed because of tourism. So did the restrictions protecting the water beneath us. We started talking as the boat cut through the darkness toward Phi Phi. I asked where he was from. “Phi Phi,” he replied with a small smile. That short answer somehow explained everything. For him, tourism was not a vacation or a temporary escape. The sea, the tourists, the coral reefs, the ferry routes, the hotels, the beach clubs, the environmental restrictions -- all of it connected directly to survival. And sitting there in the middle of the Andaman Sea, watching Phi Phi slowly glow brighter against the dark water, I realized something Bangladesh still struggles to fully understand. Tourism is not simply about having beautiful places. Tourism is about building systems around beauty. That is the real reason Thailand succeeds. As a Bangladeshi traveller, I spent much of my trip comparing Thailand’s tourism economy with the unrealized potential of Bangladesh. Because the uncomfortable truth is that Bangladesh is not lacking natural beauty. We have beaches, rivers, forests, hills, islands, archaeological heritage, and cultural richness that many countries would envy. Yet Thailand earns from tourism with confidence and sophistication, while Bangladesh still approaches tourism like an underdeveloped side sector. The difference is not nature. The difference is design. Thailand does not merely show destinations to tourists. It engineers experiences around them. A sunset becomes an economy. A beach becomes a system. A boat ride becomes memory, photography, transport, nightlife, local employment and storytelling all at once. From the moment I landed in Bangkok and later travelled through Phuket and Phi Phi, one thing became obvious: Thailand reduces friction for visitors. Airports connect efficiently to domestic flights. Hotels connect smoothly to ferries. Beach areas flow naturally into restaurants, nightlife and shopping. Convenience stores, taxis, walking routes and tourism services feel synchronized. A visitor rarely feels lost for long. Thailand understood early that tourism is not only hospitality. It is infrastructure, psychology and economic planning. The country began institutionalizing tourism decades ago. The Tourist Organization of Thailand was established in 1960 and later became the Tourism Authority of Thailand in 1979. During the Vietnam War era, Bangkok’s tourism and entertainment sectors expanded rapidly around American military rest-and-recreation demand. Hotels, nightlife, transport and service industries grew around that ecosystem. But Thailand’s real achievement was turning those early opportunities into long-term national strategy. Today, tourism remains one of Thailand’s economic pillars. Tourist spending exceeded 1.6 trillion baht in 2024, and the sector contributes roughly 10-20% of Thailand’s economy when direct and indirect impacts are included. Bangladesh’s tourism contribution to GDP, meanwhile, remains negligible despite possessing enormous natural assets. Thailand’s real achievement is also not just numbers. It is atmosphere. I understood that most clearly at night. My room in Phi Phi was directly on the beach. I could walk out of the door and, within seconds, my feet were on the sand. Yet despite the commercial activity surrounding the island, the area remained surprisingly clean and organized. There were no chaotic concrete barriers separating visitors from the sea, no overwhelming visual pollution, no feeling that nature had completely surrendered to commerce. Late at night, after walking through the glowing beach clubs and crowded tourist streets, I would slowly walk back along the shoreline toward my room. The music from the bars would gradually fade behind me. Then only the sound of the waves remained. The Andaman Sea would stretch into darkness beside me while the lights of Phi Phi flickered softly behind. Standing there in the middle of the night, I remember thinking how strange it was that a place filled with tourists and businesses could still feel this peaceful, this beautiful, almost unreal. For a moment, Phi Phi no longer felt like a tourist destination. It felt like a place imagined by someone trying to design paradise. And perhaps that is Thailand’s greatest achievement in tourism. Despite all the business, movement and commercialization, it still protects moments that feel deeply personal and emotionally real. That was also the moment I kept thinking about Bangladesh. We have Cox’s Bazar -- one of the longest natural sea beaches in the world -- yet we have not been able to transform that extraordinary advantage into a world-class tourism experience. Thailand has built a massive tourism economy around smaller beaches and islands by designing systems around them. Bangladesh possesses a far larger natural asset, but still struggles with planning, cleanliness, service quality, environmental management, transport coordination, pedestrian infrastructure, nightlife design, and international branding. In many Bangladeshi tourist areas, development still appears reactive rather than planned. Commercial expansion often arrives before waste systems, zoning enforcement, visitor management, or environmental protection. A long beach alone is not an economy. A beautiful island alone is not a destination. Nature provides the raw material. Planning transforms it into value. The comparison becomes even more urgent when thinking about environmental protection. The same boatman who spoke to me about tourists also spoke about restrictions near Maya Bay. That matters. Maya Bay, made globally famous through the film The Beach , eventually suffered severe ecological damage because of overtourism. Coral reefs deteriorated. Marine ecosystems weakened. Thai authorities later closed the bay for recovery and introduced stricter visitor regulations when reopening it. Thailand understood a difficult truth before it was too late: A destroyed paradise cannot remain profitable. Bangladesh now faces a similar challenge with Saint Martin’s Island. As Bangladesh’s only coral-bearing island, Saint Martin’s is ecologically fragile and economically important. But years of overtourism, waste, unplanned development and environmental stress have placed enormous pressure on the island. Recent restrictions and seasonal controls are important steps, but restrictions alone are not enough. Saint Martin’s requires long-term ecological planning, proper waste systems, marine protection, controlled visitor capacity, trained tourism workers, and sustainable local income models. Bangladesh must stop treating Saint Martin’s as an unlimited mass-tourism destination. It should be managed as a premium ecological asset. Lek understood this instinctively. If the sea dies, his work dies with it. That understanding is perhaps what Bangladesh needs most urgently -- not simply more tourism promotion, but a more serious understanding of tourism itself. Tourism is not just leisure. It is employment. Foreign currency. Infrastructure. Branding. Entrepreneurship. Cultural diplomacy. Rural economic growth. Soft power. Thailand learned how to convert geography into economic emotion. Bangladesh still largely stops at geography. And yet the opportunity remains enormous. If Bangladesh invests seriously in tourism planning, environmental protection, transport systems, hospitality standards and destination management, places like Cox’s Bazar, Saint Martin’s, or the hill districts could support far stronger local economies while preserving ecological value. But that requires discipline. Not slogans. As our boat finally approached Phi Phi that night, the island glowed brighter against the dark sea. Music carried softly across the shore. The plankton still flickered beside the boat. For us, it was a holiday. For “Lek” the boatman, it was work. For Thailand, it was their economy. Evan Iqram works at Transparency International (TIB).

item.title: Doing all we can to tackle mob violence. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Qwen-3-32B

The government’s consideration of a separate law to tackle mob violence is, undoubtedly, both timely and necessary. It is safe to say that the existing legal framework has proven to be inadequate in dealing with the alarming rise of mob attacks and lynchings across the country. At present, incidents of mob violence are prosecuted under scattered provisions related to murder, assault, unlawful assembly, or rioting. As legal experts have pointed out, however, mob violence itself lacks a distinct legal definition, making investigations and prosecutions unnecessarily complicated. This loophole has often allowed instigators and those spreading inflammatory rumours to walk scot-free, while only a few direct perpetrators face consequences. The government’s concern is particularly urgent because Bangladesh has witnessed a deeply troubling normalization of mob justice in recent years. Human rights organizations have documented a sharp increase in lynchings linked to allegations of theft, blasphemy, political identity, personal disputes, and online misinformation. Social media-fueled hysteria, political polarization, intolerance, and a widespread culture of impunity have all contributed to an environment where too many within the nation increasingly act as both the deciders and the upholders of justice. A new law is not the sole solution, however. Bangladesh's lack isn't merely a legal one; it is in a crisis of public trust in institutions. If perpetrators continue to believe they can evade punishment through sheer numbers or political influence, no legislation will be enough. The government must therefore pursue a different strategy: A dedicated anti-mob violence law should clearly define mob crimes, establish strict penalties for organizers and inciters, and make such offenses non-bailable in severe cases. Simultaneously, law enforcement agencies must be trained and empowered to intervene rapidly before violence escalates. Additionally, digital rumour monitoring, public awareness campaigns, and swift prosecution through fast-track tribunals are also essential. Most importantly, it must be made clear by the authorities that mob violence will not be tolerated. Only through consistent enforcement can this ingrained culture of violence be dismantled.

item.title: Who speaks for the tiger widows of Sundarbans?. [ thedailystar ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, GPT-OSS-120B

Bangladesh’s tiger widows endure stigma, poverty, and neglect while bearing conservation’s hidden human costs alone for decades. Delivered by PolitePaul service

item.title: Disconnected trade systems are eroding the gains of digitisation. [ thedailystar ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen-3-32B, GPT-OSS-20

In today’s global trading system, data moves alongside goods. Every shipment is accompanied by invoices, regulatory approvals, banking records, inspection reports, and logistical updates.
